titleStmt.title: Letter from Maurice Moore to Alice Stopford Green, 27 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Maurice Moore

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Maurice Moore (1854-1939), soldier and politician, to Alice Stopford Green (1847-1929), wife of historian John Richard Green (1837-1883). he letter relates to progress around a petition to save Sir Roger Casement (1864-1916). Moore says he 'feels really reassured because everything that can be done has been done.' Roger Casement had been arrested at Banna Strand, County Kerry on the eve of the Easter Rising. Alice Stopford Green was an Irish historian and nationalist. While not a supporter of armed rebellion, her house provided a space for leading nationalists to meet. Stopford Green had collaborated with Roger Casement on Congo Reform and, after the Easter Rising in 1916, she tried to save him from execution. Colonel Maurice George Moore, author, soldier and politician, was the younger brother of the writer George Moore. A member of the provisional committee of the Irish Volunteers in 1913, he was made the organisation's Inspector General, spending much of 1914 organising the troops in Ireland. In 1916 he collected a petition with Agnes O'Farrelly asking for a reprieve of the death sentence against Roger Casement.

msIdentifier.collection: Roger Casement Papers, Collection List No. A15, Ms. 13,088/9/9

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
lists by book post Buswells Hotel July 27th My dear Mrs Green I enclose a list of our
signatures to date for your information.
Will you please pass on (if of no
use to you) to Miss O'Farrelly
Irish School Gortahork Co Donegal so that
she may know. She is working
that part of the country & Miss Young
Clare. The Dublin Corporation passed
a resolution & sent it to me - this was
arranged by Mr Sherlock & well
managed. We did not want an
angry wrangle. I see the M.Ps went to Asquith
& presented petitions. I got Yeats letter
in Freeman && handed a copy to
an American Correspondent who cabled
(or tried to) to America. Also my letter
to Freeman he cabled. He said America
was most excited & shouting for news. I feel greatly re-assured today.
I think everything that can be done has
been done; at least as much as is
required if Gov. wish to let him off; if
they are determined the other way ten
times more would do no good. The
Unionists seem very bitter - bitter in
exact proportion as they themselves have
been involved (except Carson). The Cabinet
has disgraced itself thoroughly; Redmond
managed the showing up skilfully at all
events; he is good at that. I don't know
what the end will be. As far as I can
guess it was a deliberate attempt to
wreck the parliamentary party & rid
itself for a number of years of the
incubus of an Irish party dictating
terms. Both parties seem equally involved.
Asquith & c tried to do again what
Gladstone did to Parnell. He saw
an opportunity of smashing the party.
English politicians hated United
Irish party. After I published the Cardinal's
letter the Archbishop of Dublin sent
in his adhesion. I am sending
you today's Freeman with my letter.
I thought it better to remark on the
libels; they are being spread far & wide.
This is a good moment for it as
sympathy has risen high & people
will be glad to be re-assured. After
a reprieve would be too late. I have been writing an article on
his behaviour & its objects. Miss
Bannister has been telling everyone
over here what you told me, as
it is not a secret. Unless there
is a distinct objection I think it
ought to be published. The Independent put in our notices
but in a very condensed form.
Derry Journal puts them in full
& had a leading article. Yours sincerely Maurice Moore
